data.title: Cover Story: LTAT: A retirement fund like no other in Malaysia
data.description:
This article first appeared in The Edge Malaysia Weekly on April 7, 2025 - April 13, 2025

WHAT comes to mind when you hear “Lembaga Tabung Angkatan Tentera”?

Some may view the Armed Forces Fund Board (LTAT) as a retirement fund akin to the Employees Provident Fund (EPF) but one that’s bespoke for the Malaysian armed forces. While not entirely incorrect, this school of thought is not wholly accurate either.

Recruits who begin their career with the Malaysian Armed Forces (MAF) — be it the Malaysian Army, Royal Malaysian Navy or Royal Malaysian Air Force — pay a monthly defined contribution to LTAT under their initial 12-year employment. This comprises 10% of their monthly salary, while another contribution equal to 15% of their monthly salary would be paid into the fund by the government as the employer — much like the EPF, albeit with differing contribution rates.

Beyond the initial 12-year term, “other ranks” — enlisted personnel and non-commissioned officers (enlisted personnel who have risen through the ranks rather than being commissioned), who comprise the bulk of the armed forces and are referred to as servicemen under the Tabung Angkatan Tentera Act 1973 — can qualify for subsequent three-year extensions.

This is where LTAT’s retirement system differs from that of the EPF. Once servicemen have a 21-year verified service record, they are eligible for pension. Regular commissioned officers are pensionable with 20 years of verified service or when they reach the voluntary or compulsory retirement age. Short-term commissioned officers are pensionable at the compulsory retirement age.

The MAF’s compulsory retirement age ranges from 50 to 60 based on rank, while the voluntary retirement age ranges from 40 to 50 based on rank and gender. Other ranks or servicemen retire from the armed forces at their end-of-service date.

Once a soldier is pensionable, he still contributes to the LTAT until he is discharged from service. However, the government will no longer contribute its share of monthly contributions, and prior government contributions to the soldier’s LTAT account will be returned to government coffers.

Full withdrawal from LTAT is eligible a month after discharge from service, reaching 50 years of age or upon the contributor’s death. A retired soldier’s monthly pension, which is paid by the government, is calculated based on a fraction (differs for other ranks, regular commissioned officers and short-term commissioned officers) of their total verified months in service multiplied by the final salary.

Contributions from other ranks to LTAT have always been compulsory, while contributions from commissioned officers only became mandatory for those commissioned after Jan 1, 2016 (compulsory officers).

Of LTAT’s 117,574 total members at end-2024, 110,142 or 94% constituted other ranks or servicemen, 4,936 (4%) were compulsory officers and 2,496 (2%) were officers making voluntary contributions. The monthly contributions of voluntary contributors are capped at RM2,000.

Only 6.5% or 7,669 members are non-active members; that is, those who have retired and no longer contribute but opted to not fully withdraw their savings in LTAT.

This “hybrid” retirement system is what makes LTAT unique, says its chief executive Mohammad Ashraf Md Radzi, while explaining the government’s move back in 1973 to have a specific retirement fund for servicemen under the MAF.

“It starts off as a defined contribution and ends up with a defined benefit. With all this, liability projection is not easy.

“With the EPF, it’s age-based. Anytime you have a baseline income, you apply the ruler for age, and you already know when your liability is going to come,” says Ashraf. “With us [LTAT], we have in our portfolio a basket of contributors who are on contracts for 12, 15, 18, 21 or 26 years. On top of that, a set for officers only became compulsory in 2016. So, liability projection is anyone’s guess.”

 

Save by subscribing to us for your print and/or digital copy.

P/S: The Edge is also available on Apple's App Store and Android's Google Play.

data.title: CTOS Digital may log softer 1Q earnings but rebound in sight in 2Q — BIMB Securities
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): BIMB Securities Research anticipates a potential dip in credit reporting agency CTOS Digital Bhd's (KL:CTOS) first-quarter results, but projects a subsequent rebound in business momentum starting in the second quarter.

In a note on Thursday, the research house said that an earnings miss — if any — for the first quarter ended March 31, 2025 (1QFY2025) should be seen as a tactical buying window.  

BIMB maintained its positive outlook for CTOS for FY2025, viewing it as a period of growth driven by key catalysts expected to emerge from 2QFY2025.

The research house added that structural trends — such as the expansion of digital financial services, increasing credit participation, and sustained institutional demand for risk analytics — remain unchanged. 

Economic conditions across CTOS' key operating markets remain robust, with forecasts for gross domestic product (GDP) growth and business activity indicating continued expansion.   

The structural demand for credit — and by extension, credit data — continues to strengthen, supported by advances in digital financial inclusion and sustained domestic consumption, which remain a central driver of GDP in these economies, according to the note.

BIMB said that US is not a significant trading partner for the markets in which CTOS operates, making concerns related to potential US political developments — such as the so-called "Trump risk" — largely immaterial to the company’s outlook. 

The firm added that CTOS offers a more attractive dividend yield, and a lower price-earnings ratio compared to its industry peers. At a share price of RM1.12, the firm sees a 19% upside potential.

The stock is trading at a 23% discount to global peers on a price-to-free cash flow (P/FCF) basis, despite the company’s stronger structural growth drivers and superior projected return on equity, said the house.

BIMB cautioned that investors focusing solely on potential 1QFY2025 earnings risks may overlook the broader long-term growth story.  

“Investors overly focused on 1QFY2025 risk missing the forest for the trees,” the firm said. 

BIMB further reiterated its 'buy' recommendation for CTOS, maintaining its target price of RM1.33, based on 30 times FY2025 forecast P/FCF — broadly in line with peer benchmarks.        

In FY2024, CTOS' net profit fell by 10% to RM106.27 million, down from RM118.11 million a year ago, despite a 16.6% increase in cumulative revenue to RM304.85 million from RM261.44 million. 

Meanwhile, CTOS' net profit fell 42.2% to RM32.39 million for 4QFY2024, from RM56.01 million a year ago, largely due to higher tax expenses arising from the absence of a tax credit. Revenue for the quarter increased 5% to RM76.82 million from RM73.15 million in 4QFY2023. 

CTOS declared a fourth interim dividend of 0.99 sen per share — payable on April 30 — bringing its total dividends for FY2024 to 3.25 sen per share, versus 3.33 for FY2023. 

According to Bloomberg, 11 analysts, including BIMB, recommend 'buy', indicating a bullish sentiment. 

At the time of writing on Thursday, CTOS shares were down one sen or 0.9% at RM1.11, valuing the group at RM2.56 billion. Year to date, the stock has retreated 7.5%. 

Read also:
Absence of tax credit drags CTOS Digital’s net profit 42.2% lower in 4Q

Edited ByIsabelle Francis

data.title: Cover Story: LTAT moves forward despite challenges
data.description:
This article first appeared in The Edge Malaysia Weekly on April 7, 2025 - April 13, 2025

SHORTLY before Hari Raya Aidilfitri, Lembaga Tabung Angkatan Tentera (LTAT) announced a dividend of 5.25% for 2024 — the highest it has paid out in seven years. The total distribution of RM514.2 million made up 87.5% of the armed forces fund’s net profit for the year.

While the dividend payout is a great improvement from the 2% in 2018 and 2.5% in 2019, there is still much to be done for LTAT to return to its former glory.

The key question is whether the armed forces fund is using internally generated funds or taking on borrowings to make the dividend payout.

LTAT chief executive Mohammad Ashraf Md Radzi, who took office a year ago in April 2024, tells The Edge in an exclusive interview that the armed forces fund finances its dividend payments entirely through internally generated funds, without any reliance on borrowings or external working capital financing.

“The declared dividend is credited to members’ accounts and will only be disbursed upon withdrawal at the end of their service. LTAT does not distribute dividends in cash, thereby mitigating exposure to significant cash withdrawals related to dividend payouts. Additionally, LTAT maintains adequate liquidity at all times to meet withdrawal obligations through available cash reserves and liquid assets,” says Ashraf, who joined the armed forces fund as chief financial officer.

“Our dividend payments are prudently managed through internal financial resources, ensuring sustainable and responsible financial governance,” he adds, noting that, unlike Permodalan Nasional Bhd (PNB) which has a proprietary fund to tap into for dividends should the need arise, LTAT does not have such a fund.

Ashraf says LTAT’s asset rebalancing and diversification exercise, anchored on its Strategic Asset Allocation Framework (SSA), remains a priority as it ensures the sustainability of the fund while staying aligned with market conditions and global fund management standards. The framework was drawn up in 2020 and introduced in 2021.

LTAT’s assets under management had grown to RM13.6 billion at end-2024, from RM9.26 billion in 2020 (see table), while its reserves exceeded the RM1 billion mark, from RM45 million in 2018. Despite this, sceptics remain cynical about the armed forces fund, largely because of its chequered past.

When asked how the past year at the helm has been, Ashraf says, “The appointment [as chief executive] was effective April 1, but it doesn’t feel like it has been a year. It feels like it has been much longer.

“It [LTAT] needs a lot of work. Looking at how things were looked after previously, it certainly isn’t a two- or three-person job. It needs a management team to look after the whole thing.”

Later he adds, “Hopefully with the dividend that we announced, I will still have about a billion [ringgit] in reserves — so we will be able to handle any shocks that may come.”

Ashraf’s trepidation is understandable, considering the concerns at LTAT and the missteps over the past two years which saw his predecessor, Datuk Ahmad Nazim Abd Rahman, leave in January 2024.

While Ashraf has brought some semblance of stability to the armed forces fund, he has been at the helm for only a year. And LTAT has had four chief executives in the last seven years.

Ashraf also realises the difficulty in growing its portfolio size given the minimal increase in members each year due to the fixed size of the Malaysian armed forces — an issue that his peers at other government-linked investment companies do not encounter. 

“The way the government runs the armed forces — at about 120,000 in strength — is flat, maintained. Each year, about 7,000-odd leave, who are replaced by about 7,000. So, it’s net flat. When it comes to managing the inflows and outflows, we are also about net flat. All the withdrawals are supported by the inflows, new contributions,” he explains as he talks about the challenges he faces in running the retirement fund.

“Usually, it’s a small gap, which is very manageable by us. But that creates a different challenge for us at LTAT. Our capital is flat. If you compare with the Employees Provident Fund, Kumpulan Wang Persaraan (Diperbadankan) (KWAP) and even Lembaga Tabung Haji, all of them are operating on a net positive cash inflow each month, or even if not each month, each year for sure — whereas our capital is flat. So, I need to sweat my assets all the time.”

In 2024, LTAT saw 7,879 new members, while 7,574 members fully withdrew their retirement savings. Contributions stood at RM1.01 billion versus RM1.17 billion in withdrawals — coming out to a net withdrawal of RM161 million.

Challenges at Boustead Holdings

At the top of LTAT’s list of assets is its flagship Boustead Holdings Bhd, which it took private in June 2023 for RM703.25 million, or 85.5 sen per share for the remaining 40.58% equity interest in the company.

Barely four months later, in October 2023, the armed forces fund forked out RM1.11 billion, or RM1.55 per share, to buy out another listed entity — Boustead Plantations Bhd (BPlant) — via Boustead Holdings. This deal came about after the original plan to partner with Kuala Lumpur Kepong Bhd (KL:KLK) to jointly own the company hit a snag.

This U-turn resulted in Ahmad Nazim leaving LTAT, attributed as well to a planned restructuring being put on hold following a change at the helm at the Ministry of Defence, from Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan to current minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin. LTAT chairman General Tan Sri Raja Mohamed Affandi Raja Mohamed Noor also resigned, possibly echoing the dissatisfaction over Ahmad Nazim’s departure.

The restructuring was critical, considering that the assets of the armed forces fund and Boustead Holdings were facing financial difficulties at that point. LTAT has 54.9% stake, via Boustead Holdings, in Pharmaniaga Bhd (KL:PHARMA), a Practice Note 17 (PN17) company; a 72.38% stake in ailing Boustead Heavy Industries Corp Bhd (KL:BHIC); and a 22% stake in Affin Bank Bhd (KL:AFFIN).

LTAT and Boustead Holdings together sold nearly 27% of their stake in Affin Bank, the seventh largest banking group in the country by assets, to a Sarawak state entity last year to reduce its debts.

Also in the armed forces fund’s portfolio are Boustead Petroleum Marketing Sdn Bhd, better known as BHPetrol; the Royale Chulan hotel chain; and some parcels of land, among other assets.

BHIC, which mustered a meagre RM7.57 million in net profit on RM237.14 million in revenue for its financial year ended Dec 31, 2024 (FY2024), has been plagued by the ghost of the controversial RM9.13 billion littoral combat ship project that had to be taken over by the Ministry of Finance. As at end-2024, the company had accumulated losses of RM463.77 million.

Meanwhile, Pharmaniaga has been grappling with a restructuring exercise since falling into the PN17 category, which was brought about by a RM552.3 million impairment due to unsold Covid-19 vaccine. The large impairment dragged the group into its deepest quarterly net loss of RM664.39 million, on RM862.72 million in revenue, in the fourth quarter ended Dec 31, 2022 (see story on Page 61).

Ashraf explains his predicament. “I think throughout the Boustead [Holdings] group, one of the key challenges that we discovered when we began the transformation was the mismatch of debt, mismatch of debt profile — you have your various instruments, term loan, RC (revolving credit), sukuk … but your selection of these debt instruments is on the basis of what you use it for.

“So, within the group, what we discovered was that there were a lot of mismatches, short-term revolving credits that were utilised for purchases of assets that carried long gestation periods. So, these are the things that did not help the business, because suddenly you’re firefighting in your business operations and your profitability is going towards debt servicing.”

Boustead Holdings’ debt levels

While Boustead Holdings is asset-rich, it has been crumbling under the weight of its debts.

In November last year, Public Accounts Committee chairperson Datuk Mas Ermieyati Samsudin highlighted concerns about LTAT’s financial instability and the suspension of a restructuring plan dubbed Project Moonshot. The PAC expressed its fears that prolonged inaction could force the armed forces fund to liquidate its assets, which could result in a cash flow crunch, multiple financial challenges and erode up to 50% of its funds, thus putting nearly RM5 billion of its contributors’ money at risk.

A check with the Companies Commission of Malaysia reveals that in FY2023, Boustead Holdings had total liabilities of RM7.43 billion, of which RM6.73 billion were current liabilities due in a year. Among the reasons to take the company private is to expedite its debt restructuring exercise and get it back into shape, as being a listed company with the many processes and shareholder approvals required could slow things down.

Boustead Holdings’ sale of a 27% stake in Affin Bank to the Sarawak government brought in about RM1.78 billion, which was largely used to pare down its debts.

On Boustead Holdings’ debt levels, Ashraf says: “The debt is down to about RM1.6 billion. It was about RM3.4 billion before. So, of the RM1.6 billion, RM600 million-plus are the perpetuals (perpetual bonds), which is very expensive. That is the next, technically, we target to resolve. We can’t afford to continue having something that pays out 13% [in interest].”

Boustead Holdings’ perpetual sukuk of RM1.2 billion was approved by the Securities Commission Malaysia in mid-November 2013. The perpetual sukuk is accounted as equity because there is no contractual obligation to redeem the instrument.

From Boustead Holdings’ last annual report, which is for FY2022, the perpetual sukuk amounted to RM607 million and had rates of between 9.75% and 11.6%. It is also stated that if the company did not exercise its option to redeem the instrument at the end of fifth year, the periodic distribution rate would increase by 1.5% per annum in the sixth year. And from the seventh year onwards, the periodic distribution rate would be increased by another 1% per annum for every year thereafter, subject to a maximum of 15% per annum.

News reports have it that pilgrims’ fund Lembaga Tabung Haji is the largest holder of the perpetual sukuk, with RM175 million in the Islamic debt paper, followed by LTAT with RM150 million and KWAP with RM100 million.

Nevertheless, while LTAT is not cash-strapped, some of Boustead Holdings’ assets may be hived off. The proceeds will be used largely for reinvestment purposes and recapitalisation.

“What is strategic to us … there’s pharmaceuticals, there’s plantations … To an extent, what we have done well historically is defence, even though it’s very transactional or contractual in nature. Properties, we’re in there for the long run by virtue of our large holdings in Mutiara Damansara,” says Ashraf.

From Moonshot to Earth

At end-February, defence minister Mohamed Khaled mentioned that LTAT and Boustead Holdings would undergo a restructuring under a new plan dubbed Project Earth. In a written parliamentary reply, he said: “The objective [of Project Earth] is to enhance operational efficiency and productivity while ensuring LTAT’s subsidiaries remain competitive in their respective industries.”

In truth, this was the first time many had even heard of Project Moonshot, and it came as a surprise that it was being replaced so soon.

Ashraf says, “Over the past six months through all the PAC (Public Accounts Committee) sessions, there was a lot of news coming out from it, questions asked on Moonshot, under my predecessor [Ahmad] Nazim. What I’ve gone on to do is transition that into Project Earth, where I think I’ve grounded it in terms of the intent of what our real strategic businesses should be. And we believe that under our stable, our gem is Pharmaniaga (see accompanying story) despite its financial challenges, and the other one is [Boustead] Plantations.”

While details on Project Earth are scarce, what is known is that it is a refinement taking into consideration the need to nurture key companies with long-term benefits and growth prospects.

On how Earth came about from Moonshot, Ashraf says, “I think it’s not the case of what went wrong, but about preference — how Moonshot has transitioned into Earth. The main difference is the question of keeping Boustead Holdings. Whereas Moonshot was with the idea of shutting down Boustead Holdings. I think it’s easier said than done. When the company was carrying RM3.4 billion of debt, and even up to this point when we’ve addressed the MTNs (medium-term notes) that were due, there’s still RM1.6 billion [in debt] left. Even if we embark on maybe another round of monetisation that raises RM600 million to RM800 million, or even a billion, no lender would take too kindly to the asset-stripping of the entity where it has exposure.”

Project Earth was presented to the cabinet on Dec 13, 2024, and its guiding principles include improving operational efficiency and productivity, ensuring that subsidiaries remain competitive in their respective industries, with each operating company structured under the most optimal shareholding.

In a nutshell, Project Earth is aimed at reducing LTAT’s strategic assets to 35% and reinvesting in more liquid assets, with a target to increase the fixed income allocation to 20%, by 2028.

Boustead Plantations’ transformation

The plan to hive off BPlant and retain a small stake has changed after the company was taken private. There is a government guarantee from the finance ministry for the RM2 billion loan LTAT took to buy the 32% stake not held by Boustead Holdings.

“I think the guidance from the government is, ‘Look, you’ve held on to land-based assets for a long time. So, instead of taking the easy route of just selling it off, why don’t you take care of it?’ I think that’s the message that I understand. So I’ve then cleaned up … We’ve put in a new management team who is very hands-on, who goes down to ground … Plantations are not rocket science. They need attention. We have so many good plantation companies, both GLCs and private ones. So naturally there is talent, there is technology available for us to tap into. So what it needs is attention, the right level of attention from management all the way up to the number one,” says Ashraf.

BPlant owns and manages 42 oil palm estates and 10 palm oil mills across Malaysia. It has a total land bank of 97,500ha, of which 74% is planted.

In early November last year, Eco World Development Group Bhd (KL:ECOWLD), via its 81%-owned subsidiary Mutiara Balau Sdn Bhd, sought to buy 10 parcels of land measuring 847.25 acres in Semenyih, Selangor, for RM742.41 million from BPlant. It is looking at a new mixed-use development called Eco Forest 2, which will have an estimated gross development value of RM4.6 billion.

“We are assisted by having a few pockets of estates that are already mature for development, that are no longer suitable for plantations. Recently, what we did with EcoWorld gives us the monetisation that we need to reinvest in catching up on replanting and all the fertilising and cleaning up of the estates,” says Ashraf.

“What we need to do is concentrate on nurturing it [BPlant] over the next two to three years. I think we will find ourselves in a good position to relist it.”

The recent success of Johor Plantations Group Bhd (KL:JPG) could have been the catalyst for this plan. Johor Plantations’ initial public offering in March 2022 raised RM1 billion, with the public portion oversubscribed by 2.18 times.

Whether Ashraf will be at the helm of LTAT to witness the changes coming to fruition remains to be seen. But the changes under Project Earth, if implemented, could be a game changer for the armed forces fund. 

 

Save by subscribing to us for your print and/or digital copy.

P/S: The Edge is also available on Apple's App Store and Android's Google Play.

data.title: Malaysia delays LNG shipments due to outage at export plant, Bloomberg reports
data.description:
(April 17): Malaysia is delaying liquefied natural gas (LNG) shipments to buyers due to an outage at its massive export facility, threatening to curb supply in Asia.

Petronas, Malaysia’s state-owned exporter, has requested delays for more than five scheduled shipments affected by the outage, said the traders, asking not to be identified as they were not authorised to speak with media.

At least three production trains were offline at Petronas’ Bintulu export facility, according to Kayrros, which gathers operational information via satellites. The outage was detected on April 9, it said.

Petroliam Nasional Bhd, as the state-owned company is formally known, didn’t respond to a request for comment.

This is the latest in a string of outages across Asia, which could support spot prices of the power plant and industrial fuel. Brunei LNG and the Ichthys facility in Australia were forced to reduce output earlier this month for unplanned maintenance.

Uploaded by Tham Yek Lee

data.title: Ex-Goldman banker Roger Ng could testify in Najib’s 1MDB-Tanore criminal trial
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): Former Goldman Sachs banker Roger Ng Chong Hwa might testify in former prime minister Datuk Seri Najib Razak’s 1Malaysia Development Bhd-Tanore (1MDB-Tanore) criminal trial.

Deputy public prosecutor Kamal Bahrin Omar told trial judge Datuk Collin Lawrence Sequerah that the prosecution had received Najib’s application on Monday, April 14.

“The prosecution can make arrangements to facilitate Ng to come to the court as a witness for a specific date. We could make the arrangement, subject to the prosecution giving the particulars of the arrangement,” he said on Thursday.

Najib’s lead counsel Tan Sri Muhammad Shafee Abdullah informed the court that they had submitted the subpoena to call Ng as a witness, and it was addressed to the director general of the Witness Protection Division of the Prime Minister’s Department, Khairul Anwar Zakaria @ Abdul Hamid.

“The defence wants to call Ng, and has no interest in calling the director general. However, as the prosecution is willing to cooperate, we need time to interview Ng first, before deciding on calling him as a (defence) witness,” Shafee said.

Following this, Kamal Bahrin said the prosecution is withdrawing its application to object to the calling of the Witness Protection Division director general.

Sequerah then struck out the prosecution’s objection.

Ng, a Malaysian, was sentenced by a court in the US in 2023 with 10 years’ imprisonment after being found guilty of conspiring to violate US anti-bribery laws and conspiring to launder money with regard to 1MDB.

He was sentenced on March 11, 2023, by US district judge Margo Brodie, after jurors found him guilty of helping his former Goldman Sachs boss, Tim Leissner, embezzle money from 1MDB.

However, Ng has been back in Malaysia since October 2023, through an arrangement with the US Department of Justice (DOJ).

Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail has said that the DOJ agreed to repatriate Ng for authorities in Malaysia to continue investigations into 1MDB or Goldman Sachs.

High Court registrar called over Ng’s DNAA

Following the development, High Court deputy registrar Nurul Ain Hamzah, the defence’s 21st witness, was called by Shafee to testify over Ng’s charges from the Sessions Court to the High Court.

Nurul Ain said that on Dec 17, 2018, they received approval from then-attorney general Tan Sri Tommy Thomas to charge Ng for Securities Commission Malaysia offences.

The case, she added, was then transferred to the High Court, and then Ng was granted a discharge not amounting to an acquittal (DNAA) on Jan 9, 2023, by judge Datuk Muhammad Jamil Hussin.

Following that, Shafee asked for the proceeding notes that led to Jamil granting the DNAA. However, Nurul Ain said the notes could only be issued by the High Court.

After a short break, Nurul Ain said the defence should make a formal application before Jamil to request for the court recording on Jan 9, 2023.

“Following that, they can request the recording and transcript of the proceedings,” she said.

When asked by Shafee, the witness said this could be done by next week, and the court could also endorse and verify the court documents related to the matter.

Najib is on trial on four counts of abuse of power and 21 counts of money laundering of a sum of RM2.27 billion of 1MDB funds.

The former prime minister is currently serving his jail sentence after being found guilty of charges in relation to SRC International Sdn Bhd, a former subsidiary of 1MDB.

The 1MDB-Tanore trial resumes next Monday.

Read also:
Ex-Goldman banker Roger Ng gets 10 years' jail over 1MDB scandal
Former Goldman banker Roger Ng expected to return on Saturday night to help in 1MDB probe — Saifuddin Nasution
Roger Ng given DNAA while Malaysian court awaits conclusion of his American trial

Edited ByAniza Damis

data.title: Stocks with momentum on April 17, 2025: Leform and HPP Holdings
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): theedgemalaysia.com highlighted two stocks with momentum at Bursa Malaysia's noon break on Thursday.

The stock with positive momentum was:

  • Leform Bhd (KL:LEFORM) — up half a sen or 3.33% to 15.5 sen

The stock with negative momentum was:

  • HPP Holdings Bhd (KL:HPPHB) — up half a sen or 0.99% to 51 sen

The list of stocks with momentum is generated using a proprietary mathematical algorithm highlighting stocks with a build-up in trading volume and price. The algorithm differentiates between stocks that exhibit positive (+ve) momentum and negative (-ve) momentum.  

This list is not a buy or sell recommendation. It merely tells you which stocks are seeing higher-than-normal volume and price movements.

The share price may move up or down from this point. But the “+ve” (suggesting a rising price trend on volume) and “-ve” (suggesting a falling price trend on volume) indicators should give readers a better idea of what the market is buying and when to sell. Note also that momentum generally only persists for a short period of time.  

However, each stock has an accompanying fundamental score and valuation score to help readers evaluate the attractiveness of the stocks if they want to ride the momentum.

For more detailed financial information and reports on the above-mentioned stocks, please subscribe to AbsolutelyStocks at www.absolutelystocks.com

Edited ByJason Ng

data.title: Amro names IMF deputy director Dong He as chief economist
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): The Asean+3 Macroeconomic Research Office (Amro) announced the appointment of Dong He as its new chief economist effective June 9, 2025.

He will succeed Hoe Ee Khor, whose term concludes on May 26, 2025, according to a statement from the multilateral think tank based in Singapore. He will be responsible for leading Amro’s country and regional surveillance initiatives, along with overseeing related research activities.

Amro is an international organisation aimed at promoting macroeconomic and financial stability across the Asean+3 region, comprising 10 Asean member states along with China, Japan, and South Korea.

Core functions include conducting macroeconomic surveillance, supporting regional financial cooperation, and providing technical assistance to its members.

He, who holds a PhD in economics from the University of Cambridge, is currently the deputy director of the Monetary and Capital Markets Department at the International Monetary Fund (IMF).

Prior to his role at the IMF, He held senior positions at the Hong Kong Monetary Authority, including executive director, and served as director of the Hong Kong Institute for Monetary Research.

Edited ByJason Ng

data.title: US tariffs sparked rush to get bitcoin mining gear out of Asia
data.description:
(April 17): For about a week, bitcoin mining firms in Southeast Asia faced an all-consuming deadline: midnight, April 9.

“That time, for the rest of my life, is going to be absolutely etched into my memory,” said Christopher Berschel, president of Sealion Cargo, a Canadian freight forwarding business.

US President Donald Trump had just unveiled an unexpectedly punitive reciprocal tariff plan, slapping duties of 36%, 32% and 24% on Thailand, Indonesia and Malaysia, respectively. All three have become hubs for the assembly and distribution of crypto mining machines. US-based buyers of these so-called “rigs” faced a major financial hit — unless Berschel and his crew could get them out by the April 9 cutoff.

In the frenzy that ensued, Sealion helped charter five aircraft while overseeing dozens of trucks and barges to ship US$330 million (RM1.46 billion) worth of equipment from Thailand, Malaysia and Indonesia to the US.

“With over US$80 million in potential tariff exposure at stake, every flight, truck, and pallet we touched had one mission: beat the tariff clock,” Berschel said.

Then, on April 9, Trump announced a 90-day pause on the higher tariffs set to hit dozens of trading partners, rendering the entire episode somewhat pointless, albeit costly. Miners spent as much as four times what they typically might on airfreight and perhaps 20 times the cost of ocean shipping, according to a person familiar with the matter who asked not to be identified as the costs are confidential.

What ultimately turned out to be an unnecessary race to beat the tariff deadline underscores how challenging — and expensive — it’s become for American companies to rework their global supply chains in order to avoid financial pain caused by Trump’s ever-shifting trade policies. For crypto mining, the tariff threats are especially disruptive since there are few US-made machines that can perform the type of high-performance computing needed to make money in the competitive industry.

It’s all creating a business environment that starkly contradicts the crypto-friendly president’s pledge on the campaign trail to buttress American mining companies such that bitcoin is “made in the USA”.

Crypto miners depend heavily on Chinese suppliers like Bitmain Technologies Ltd which, while Beijing-based, routes a lot of machinery through Southeast Asia. Buyers in the US have already faced delays receiving deliveries of new mining equipment since Trump’s election victory amid heightened scrutiny of Bitmain.

Taras Kulyk, CEO at mining hardware supplier and consultant Synteq Digital, said the company has fielded “a lot of inbounds about finding capacity outside the USA over the last two weeks now”, adding that it would be “extremely helpful if the tariff exemptions could be applied to our hardware and infrastructure as well”.

Even so, it’s unclear how long those exemptions will last. While the US revealed over the weekend that it was excluding a range of electronics from 145% tariffs on China and a 10% flat rate around the globe, Trump later downplayed the exemptions as a procedural step in his push to remold US trade. He said on Monday that he would still apply tariffs to phones, computers and popular consumer electronics, and that his decision would come soon.

US-listed Bitfufu Inc will remain profitable even if hit by the 24% duty on Malaysian imports, its chairman and CEO Leo Lu said. “That said, we still hope there will be ways to ease the tariff burden, bring more vitality to the industry and attract more investment into the US mining sector,” Lu added.

Global impact

The race against the clock triggered by Trump’s tariffs wasn’t confined to Asia. Vishnu Mackenchery, director of global logistics and services at Compass Mining Inc, had to rush 3,000 units over the Canadian border after the April 2 announcement — all while sorting out which parts originated in China, Thailand and elsewhere.

“It’s difficult to make business decisions in this type of climate because of how fluid a lot of the tariffs have been,” Mackenchery said. “It’s changing not even on a day-to-day basis — it’s changing on an hourly basis.”

Still, Southeast Asia was the heart of the storm. There, the urgency was palpable.

Bitmain has a factory under the name PT Aohai Technology Indonesia on the island of Batam — part of a free trade zone in the region — where it assembles and ships its products, according to two people familiar with the matter. Bitmain didn’t respond to a request for comment.

EES Freight Services arranged, at just one day’s notice, for a barge full of mining equipment to sail from the Indonesian island to nearby Singapore, where the cargo was loaded onto planes and flown to the US, according to Heru Winata, the firm’s operations director. EES also chartered a plane to fly machines from Batam to Kuala Lumpur, but encountered delays upon discovering there were no forklifts at the island airport and hence no way to load the aircraft.

Lauren Lin, head of hardware at Luxor Technology, said the firm has brought about 3,000 units — its own and its partners’ — from Thailand to the US since the April 2 announcement.

World Air Logistics managing director Worawut Pakdeesattayaphong had to coordinate 57 truck trips to and from a warehouse near Bangkok to cram three chartered planes with crypto rigs.

Two of the planes took off on April 7 and the third — after what Pakdeesattayaphong described as “non-stop operations” throughout the night — departed on April 8, just in what everyone at that point believed to be the nick of time.

Uploaded by Chng Shear Lane

data.title: Asia-Pacific banking sector remains mostly resilient amid US tariff risks — Fitch Ratings
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): Fitch Ratings is maintaining its mostly neutral outlook for the banking sector across several Asia-Pacific economies, including Malaysia, noting that they are more resilient to a higher US tariff regime.

In a statement, the rating agency said this is due to the economies’ generally lower direct export exposure to the US.

Although it had initially anticipated broadly stable operating conditions across most Asia-Pacific banking systems this year, a surge in US tariffs could threaten this assumption in some markets.

"The effect of the trade war on specific banking systems in the region will depend on final tariff outcomes, their impact on local economic growth, banks’ exposure to vulnerable sectors, and the potential for changes in fiscal, monetary or credit policy," it said.

Its current neutral outlook for the Korean, Taiwanese and Thai banking sectors in 2025 could move to ‘deteriorating’ if there are substantial further increases in US tariffs beyond the 10 per cent tariff imposed on all US trade partners on April 2, or if the hit to their economic growth is more severe than expected, it said.

“This could occur if the US moves ahead with the higher country-specific ‘reciprocal tariffs’ that are currently paused, for example, or because of higher tariffs on sectors such as electronics (including semiconductors),” said Fitch Ratings.

It added that bank asset quality in specific sectors could be adversely affected by additional US tariff hikes or indirect trade effects.

"For example, banks’ performance could weaken if China’s economic growth slows due to the tariffs, hitting demand for exports from their home markets.

"The trade war could also prompt national authorities to cut policy interest rates faster than we had assumed, which would lower banking sector net interest margins (NIM) in most markets," it said.

Fitch Ratings added that any changes in sector outlooks should have little impact on its banking system operating environment scores, which remain relatively stable, although China’s would potentially face the most downward pressure.

Uploaded by Lam Seng Fatt

data.title: Ford Motor may hike prices if Trump's auto tariffs persist, memo says
data.description:
(April 16): Ford Motor may raise prices on its new vehicles if US President Donald Trump's auto tariffs continue, according to a memo sent to dealers on Wednesday that was reviewed by Reuters.

The Dearborn, Michigan, automaker offered deep discounts across its lineup starting earlier this month through June 2. The programme will continue, but pricing on new models produced in May and beyond may change, the memo said.

Automotive News first reported news of the memo.

"Customers will have a lot of choices, and we have plenty of inventory to choose from through June 2. The tariff situation is dynamic and we continue to evaluate the potential impact of tariff actions," a Ford spokesman said, adding that if prices changed on vehicles produced in May, customers likely would not see an increase until early July.

Both US and foreign automakers have rushed to alleviate consumer concerns about tariffs inflating costs at dealerships by offering price assurances and substantial discounts.

While the short-term effects of tariffs have driven up sales, US automakers have privately warned lawmakers that they will be forced to drastically raise prices if the tariffs remain in place.

“In the absence of material changes to the tariff policy as articulated to date, we anticipate the need to make vehicle pricing adjustments in the future, which is expected to happen with May production,” Ford executive Andrew Frick said in the memo.

Ford will not increase the sticker price on any vehicle currently in inventory at Ford and Lincoln dealers, the memo said.

An analysis by the Center for Automotive Research published earlier this month revealed that Trump's 25% tariffs on automotive imports, implemented on April 3, will escalate costs for US automakers by about US$108 billion in 2025.

Ford is among the best-placed automaker to weather tariffs, as it produces about 80% of its US-sold vehicles domestically.

Earlier this week, Trump had floated the idea that he was considering a modification to the auto-related tariffs, suggesting he might grant exemptions on levies already in place.

Uploaded by Siow Chen Ming

data.title: Tech giants Tencent and Douyin to help China's exporters pivot to domestic market
data.description:
BEIJING (April 17): Chinese tech giants Tencent and Douyin, TikTok's sister app in China, have in the past two days launched programmes to help Chinese exporters sell their goods domestically, amid an escalating US-China trade war.

Tencent said on Thursday its programme aimed to create 100 billion yuan (US$13.7 billion) in sales for China's beleaguered export-dependent firms by helping them set up domestic-facing operations, as well as tap Southeast Asian markets.

Tencent and Douyin, which operate large e-commerce platforms, are the latest Chinese conglomerates to announce measures to help the country's exporters, which have been frozen out of the US market as a result of high tariffs imposed by the Trump administration on all Chinese imported goods.

Uploaded by Siow Chen Ming

data.title: Shein, Temu to get pricier as Trump cracks down on cheap imports
data.description:
(April 17): Chinese e-marketplace Temu and fast-fashion retailer Shein will raise prices next week as US President Donald Trump's sweeping tariffs and crackdown on imports push up costs for the companies known for their budget offerings.

In nearly identical letters to customers this week, the two companies said they will be increasing prices starting April 25 and encouraged shoppers to purchase "now at today's rates".

Shein and Temu, sellers of everything from toys to smartphones, had grown rapidly in the US thanks in part to the "de minimis" exemption that allowed duty-free entry for merchandise priced below US$800, enabling the companies to keep prices low.

However, a recent executive order signed by Trump closes the trade loophole and goes into effect on May 2.

"Due to recent changes in global trade rules and tariffs, our operating expenses have gone up. To keep offering the products you love without compromising on quality, we will be making price adjustments starting April 25, 2025," the statements from both companies said.

Shein dresses are currently priced between US$6 and US$91 on its website, while Temu sells them at anywhere between US$2.48 and US$210 online.

Temu and Shein did not immediately reply to Reuters requests for additional comments.

Uploaded by Siow Chen Ming

data.title: Malaysia’s richest just got wealthier thanks to stronger ringgit — Forbes
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): Malaysia’s richest just got richer thanks to the stronger ringgit.

The collective wealth of Malaysia’s top 50 tycoons rose to US$90 billion (RM396.9 billion) in 2025 from US$83.4 billion in the previous year thanks to a 5% appreciation in the local currency in the past 12 months, according to a statement from the Forbes magazine.

Robert Kuok, aged 101, was still Malaysia's richest individual with a net worth of US$11.4 billion, leading an agribusiness-to-hotels empire under his Kuok Group.

Tan Sri Quek Leng Chan, the patriarch of the Hong Leong Group, remained second with his fortune rising by US$1 billion to US$9.8 billion, driven by strong performance across the conglomerate that spans from banks to cement and from real estate to food.

The Teh siblings, heirs to the late Tan Sri Teh Hong Piow of Public Bank Bhd (KL:PBBANK), held onto third place with their collective wealth growing 9% to US$5.9 billion.

The list was compiled based on shareholding and financial data from various sources including stock exchanges, company reports, analysts, and government records. Net worths were calculated using stock prices and exchange rates as of market close on April 7, 2025.

The ranking lists both individual and family fortunes, including those shared among relatives and may differ from the World’s Billionaires List, which includes individual fortunes with net worths as of March 7.

Overall, 30 of the 50 wealthiest Malaysians are better off from a year ago. Among them are Tan Sri Paul Koon Poh Keong and his siblings running Press Metal Aluminium Holdings Bhd (KL:PMETAL), who moved up one spot to the fourth place after racking up a net worth of US$5.4 billion.

New additions to the list of billionaires include Lee Thiam Wah, the founder of 99 Speed Mart Retail Holdings Bhd (KL:99SMART), at number eight with US$3.5 billion after the listing of the country’s largest minimart chain.

Edited ByJason Ng

data.title: SPB Development delays Main Market IPO launch to unspecified date
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): SPB Development Bhd has delayed the launch of its initial public offering (IPO) on the Main Market to an unspecified date due to the current market uncertainties.

Originally scheduled for April 21, the delay followed a “thoughtful assessment” by the management team, according to a notice from the property developer’s external public relations firm sent on Thursday.

The move is aimed at “ensuring the company’s market entry takes place under conditions that support long-term value creation for its investors and stakeholders”, the notice said.

The delay comes at a time of volatile stock markets amid escalating international trade tensions and uncertainties over US tariff policy following multiple flip-flops and other news headlines.

SPB Development followed the footsteps of Cuckoo International (MAL) Bhd, which decided last week to postpone its listing by two months. Cuckoo International has begun taking orders from investors and has offered to refund investors who seek to withdraw from the IPO.

Bursa Malaysia has hosted seven IPOs since March, and all but one flopped on their first day of listing amid the market turmoil.  

SPB Development still has time before the regulatory approval for its listing lapses. Under listing regulations, a company must complete its IPO within six months of approval, which the company secured and announced in February.

The Melaka-based developer is seeking funds to acquire more land among others, according to its draft prospectus posted in October 2024. Part of the proceeds will also be used as working capital for upcoming residential development phases in Kedah and Selangor estimated to cost RM210 million.

An offer for sale in conjunction with the IPO will also raise some cash for executive chairman Datuk Yap Pit Kian, who will be selling down his existing shares.

Hong Leong Investment Bank is the IPO’s principal adviser, bookrunner and underwriter, while Sierac Corporate Advisers is the financial adviser.

Read also:
Property developer SPB gets SC nod for Main Market listing
Melaka real estate firm SPB Development files for IPO on Main Market
Cuckoo International delays Bursa listing to June amid market rout

Edited ByJason Ng

data.title: DigitalBridge’s sale of Southeast Asian telecom towers is said to stall, Bloomberg reports
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): DigitalBridge Group Inc’s plans to sell its Southeast Asian telecom tower operator are stalling amid valuation concerns, according to people familiar with the matter.

While work on a potential sale of closely held EdgePoint Infrastructure has paused, US-listed DigitalBridge is still evaluating options and could revisit the plans later, the people said, asking not to be identified because the deliberations are private. The owner could also pursue a breakup or piecemeal transactions instead of a full sale, the people said.

The potential sale of EdgePoint had drawn interest from investment firms including I Squared Capital, but the firms were unable to reach an agreement on valuation, Bloomberg News has reported. DigitalBridge had originally sought a valuation of as much as US$4 billion (RM17.64 billion) for the whole business, people familiar with the matter said in September.

A representative for DigitalBridge declined to comment.

Market volatility and geopolitical uncertainty are also affecting transactions globally.

Global investors have been eyeing digital infrastructure assets as they seek to tap into increasing demand for technology while looking for stable returns. Blackstone Inc last year bought Australian data-centre operator AirTrunk in a deal valuing the firm at about US$16 billion.

EdgePoint is a telecommunications tower company based in Singapore and has operations in Indonesia, Malaysia and the Philippines. EdgePoint is also backed by Abu Dhabi Investment Authority.

DigitalBridge managed US$96 billion of digital-infrastructure assets, including data centres, cell towers and fiber networks as of the end of December, its website shows. Its shares have dropped 31% this year, giving it a market value of about US$1.4 billion.

Uploaded by Chng Shear Lane

data.title: Exim Bank strengthens Malaysia’s global trade with RM87.8 mil financing for Pertama Ferroalloys
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): Export-Import Bank of Malaysia Bhd (Exim Bank) has strengthened Malaysia’s global trade with the signing of a US$20 million (RM87.8 million) supplier financing facility agreement with Pertama Ferroalloys Sdn Bhd to support the expansion of the company’s production capacity in Bintulu, Sarawak.

In a statement, Exim Bank said the strategic partnership highlighted its ongoing commitment to boosting Malaysia’s trade competitiveness on the global stage.

The financing facility is expected to help Pertama Ferroalloys scale its operations internationally while reinforcing local supply chain integration and driving economic value creation.

Located in Sarawak, Pertama Ferroalloys operates Malaysia’s first large-scale manganese alloys and ferrosilicon plant.

“This initiative complements Exim Bank’s sustainability agenda, which includes a target of RM1.5 billion in sustainability financing by 2027 through its sustainability-linked products and services,” the bank said in a statement.

The signing ceremony took place at the Malaysia Pavilion, World Expo 2025 in Osaka, Japan, with Exim Bank Malaysia president and chief executive officer Nurbayu Kasim Chang and Pertama Ferroalloys chairman Hirotaka Suzuki.

The event was witnessed by the Ministry of Investment, Trade, and Industry (Miti) secretary-general Datuk Hairil Yahri Yaacob and Pertama Ferroalloys board director and general manager Yuki Nakamura.

Nurbayu said Pertama Ferroalloys’ presence in Malaysia has generated strong backward linkages, stimulating demand for local raw materials, services, and infrastructure in supporting the global supply chain for ferroalloys, an essential material in steel production used across the construction, automotive, energy, and infrastructure sectors.

“The company also fosters local empowerment, with 87% of its workforce comprising Malaysian employees,” she said.

Meanwhile, Yuki said, “This collaboration will serve as a powerful testament to what can be achieved when vision aligns with decisive action, echoing the spirit of the post-Covid-19 Development Strategy (PCDS) 2030.”

Beyond industrial output, Pertama Ferroalloys continues to make a meaningful impact through its 300-hectare bamboo plantation project launched in November 2023.

The bamboo plantation project actively involves local communities and supports Sarawak’s green development goals under PCDS 2030. It is designed to support carbon sequestration and the production of bamboo charcoal.

In 2024, Exim Bank facilitated 144 Malaysian exporters, amounting to a total financial support of RM3.1 billion through credit takaful and business financing, enabling them to venture into new markets.

To date, 67 exporters have been supported specifically through credit takaful, with exposure in 48 countries, primarily in Asia.

Uploaded by Lam Seng Fatt

data.title: DeepSeek poses ‘profound’ security threat, US House panel claims
data.description:
(April 17): Chinese artificial intelligence firm DeepSeek is a “profound threat” to US national security, a bipartisan House committee said, urging Nvidia Corp to hand over information on sales of chips that the startup may have used to develop its breakthrough chatbot model.

The House Select Committee on China alleged in a report Wednesday that DeepSeek’s ties to Chinese government interests “are significant”, citing corporate filings obtained by the panel. Lawmakers claimed that DeepSeek’s founder, Liang Wenfeng, controls the firm alongside the High-Flyer Quant hedge fund in an “integrated ecosystem” linked to state-linked hardware distributors and Chinese research institute Zhejiang Lab.

“Although it presents itself as just another AI chatbot, offering users a way to generate text and answer questions, closer inspection reveals that the app siphons data back to the People’s Republic of China (PRC), creates security vulnerabilities for its users, and relies on a model that covertly censors and manipulates information pursuant to Chinese law,” the committee said in the report.

The panel said its inquiry found that DeepSeek is powered by “tens of thousands of chips”, and the firm may have circumvented US export controls in acquiring them. Pointing to information from analytics firm SemiAnalysis, lawmakers said DeepSeek has at least 60,000 Nvidia processors, with orders for “thousands” more of the company’s H20 chips.

Committee Chairman John Moolenaar, a Republican from Michigan, and the panel’s top Democrat, Representative Raja Krishnamoorthi, separately asked Nvidia for information on customers from 11 Asian countries that bought more than 499 AI chips since 2020. In a letter sent to Nvidia Wednesday, the lawmakers singled out from Malaysia and Singapore, as well as the entities ultimately using the chips. The company has until April 30 to respond.

Nvidia said in a statement that it follows “the government’s directions to the letter” when it comes to US export restrictions. The Santa Clara, California-based company added that in the case of Singapore, its “products are shipped to other locations, including the United States and Taiwan, not to China”.

DeepSeek didn’t immediately respond to a request for comment.

Chinese embassy spokesperson Liu Pengyu rejected the committee’s claims as “groundless”. In a statement, Liu said that “the Chinese government attaches great importance to data privacy and security and protects them in accordance with the law. It has never required, nor will it require, companies or individuals to illegally collect or store data”.

The committee’s report on DeepSeek, combined with its pressure on Nvidia, mark an escalation in US government scrutiny of Chinese technology ambitions that some believe pose national security concerns. DeepSeek, founded in 2023, rose to prominence earlier this year when it released an open-source AI model that can mimic the way humans reason, prompting questions in Washington about how the firm achieved its breakthrough.

DeepSeek has said it outperformed some leading AI companies on certain benchmarks and claimed it trained the model for US$5.6 million (RM24.71 million), a far lower cost than US competitors. It sparked a sell-off in tech stocks and upended the assumption that developing cutting-edge AI would require billions of dollars and enormous computing capacity.

The panel’s report emerged after Nvidia reported that it would incur writedowns of as much as US$5.5 billion in the current quarter owing to new restrictions from the Trump administration on sales in China of its H20 chip, a device tailored to comply with previous US export curbs.

The panel, referring to findings from cybersecurity researchers at Feroot Security, also said that Hangzhou-based DeepSeek collects extensive user data that it transmits “through back-end infrastructure connected to China Mobile”, a Chinese telecom provider banned from the US market in 2019.

In its report, which cited testimony from OpenAI, the committee claimed that DeepSeek likely used “unlawful” distillation techniques, whereby one AI model uses the outputs of another for training purposes to develop similar capabilities.

OpenAI told the panel that DeepSeek employees “circumvented guardrails” to extract reasoning outputs and accelerate development at a lower cost and claimed the Chinese startup used its models “to grade model responses and filter and transform training data”. The ChatGPT maker also said it believed “DeepSeek likely also used leading open-source AI models to create high-quality synthetic data”.

In January, Bloomberg reported Microsoft Corp and OpenAI were investigating whether data output from OpenAI’s technology was obtained in an unauthorised manner by a group linked to DeepSeek.

The committee recommended expanding export controls and imposing federal procurement prohibitions on AI models from China.

President Donald Trump has called DeepSeek’s progress a “good” thing and said the release of the AI assistant from a Chinese startup “should be a wake-up call for our industries that we need to be laser-focused on competing to win”.

Uploaded by Chng Shear Lane

data.title: Malaysia Aviation Group posts RM54m net profit for FY2024, its second straight annual profit
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR (April 17): Malaysia Aviation Group Bhd (MAG), the parent company of national carrier Malaysia Airlines Bhd, has posted a net profit of RM54 million for the financial year ended Dec 31, 2024 (FY2024), its second consecutive annual profit.

This was despite a drastic 18% capacity cut across its network in the fourth quarter of 2024 that erased most of the gains it made in the first three quarters of 2024. The reduction affected a total of 6,388 flights and one million customers, said MAG group managing director Datuk Captain Izham Ismail at a media briefing on MAG 2024 annual performance on Thursday.

The RM54 million net profit, however, was down 93% from RM766.19 million in FY2023.

"This (FY2024 performance) was achieved despite any capital injection from (MAG's largest shareholder) Khazanah Nasional Bhd," said Izham.

More to come

Edited ByKang Siew Li

data.title: Vista buys Petronas oil stake in US$1.5 bil Argentina deal
data.description:
(April 17): Vista Energy, the second-biggest crude producer in Argentina’s burgeoning Vaca Muerta shale patch, has bought Petronas’ 50% stake in an oil field there for about US$1.5 billion (RM6.62 billion).

Vista will pay US$900 million in cash now for the acquisition in the Amarga Chica area of the basin, a third of which is funded with a bank loan from Banco Santander SA. Petronas will also receive about a 7% stake in Vista, worth some US$300 million. Vista will pay the remaining amount in two instalments in 2029 and 2030.

The Argentine oil producer is “adding a high-quality block at an attractive price”, Jefferies analysts said in a note to investors. They reiterated their recommendation to buy Vista’s shares, which finished the trading day in New York almost 11% higher, the biggest jump since November 2023 when libertarian President Javier Milei was voted into power.

La Amarga Chica has lower production costs than Vista’s current portfolio and sits next to its flagship Bajada de Palo Oeste field, “potentially bringing sizeable synergies”, the analysts wrote.

The deal all but cements the exit of Malaysia’s Petronas from Argentina after it withdrew from a liquefied natural gas venture with state-run YPF SA. It’s also another sign that development in the Vaca Muerta will increasingly be spearheaded by homegrown drillers, rather than multinationals: Exxon Mobil Corp sold its Argentine shale oil portfolio last year and TotalEnergies SE is looking to do the same.

YPF, which owns the other 50% of La Amarga Chica, will continue as the operator.

Shareholders of Vista — founded by SLB board member Miguel Galuccio, who lured Petronas to the field a decade ago when he was running YPF — gave management the green light last month to pursue an acquisition and finance it with debt or a stock issuance.

Vista is expanding at an uncertain time for the global oil industry as prices fall amid Donald Trump’s trade war. Galuccio warned several weeks ago that management would consider budget cuts if the light Medanito crude produced in the Vaca Muerta, which closely tracks Brent, drops below US$55.

But there are also tailwinds for Argentine shale as Milei eased capital controls this week that have long suppressed investments and as pipeline build-outs take shape.

The deal will provide rapid growth for Vista. Before the acquisition, it had been targeting daily output of up to 100,000 barrels this year, an increase from 70,000 in 2024, through a plan to invest more than US$1 billion. With La Amarga Chica, it immediately gets a 40,000-barrel boost and will need to ramp up its capital expenditures.

Read also:
Petronas keeps mum on news of its possible exit from Argentina
Petronas evaluating exit from Argentina shale oil venture, Bloomberg reports

Uploaded by Chng Shear Lane

data.title: Nvidia curbs and ASML warning spur global tech-stock rout
data.description:
(April 17): Technology stocks sank as new US government restrictions on the export of Nvidia Corp chips to China and a disappointing report from ASML Holding NV dimmed the outlook for the semiconductor sector, wiping out more than US$200 billion (RM882.39 billion) in market value for the two companies alone.

Nvidia fell 6.9% after the chipmaker warned it will report around US$5.5 billion in related charges during the fiscal first quarter. Dutch chip-equipment maker ASML slid 7.1% in US trading. The selloff in semiconductor stocks over the past three months has already wiped out about US$2 trillion in market capitalisation.

President Donald Trump’s administration has barred Nvidia from selling its H20 chip in China, an escalation of Washington’s tech battle with Beijing. Advanced Micro Devices Inc also said the export control applies to its MI308 products, and that it sees a change up to US$800 million on account. AMD fell 7.4%.

ASML later added to investor anxiety by posting orders that missed expectations and saying that it doesn’t know how to quantify the impact of recent tariff announcements.

“While ASML delivered on the revenue front, it has left investors disappointed at a time when uncertainty will punish such results,” said Ben Barringer, a technology analyst at Quilter Cheviot. Coupled with the latest export restrictions on Nvidia chips, “all of this just adds to the multitude of headaches facing tech CEOs right now, with no sign of the difficulty ending soon.”

The latest developments show how tariffs are already wreaking havoc on global companies. The Nasdaq 100 Index fell 3% on Wednesday, and the Philadelphia Stock Exchange Semiconductor Index dropped 4.1%. Losses accelerated in afternoon trading following comments from Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, who warned about trade tensions and said the central bank isn’t in a rush to reduce interest rates.

Trump’s trade war is prompting economists to scale back their forecasts for GDP growth worldwide, casting doubt over the outlook for everything from smartphone demand to computing.

Even before Washington slapped additional tariffs on much of the world — only to roll them back shortly after — analysts had questioned whether big tech firms from Microsoft Corp to Meta Platforms Inc will continue to buy Nvidia chips at the same pace in 2025.

A bigger-than-expected drop in ASML’s bookings failed to alleviate concerns over a potential slowdown in AI demand. Investor focus will now turn to results from key customer Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co on Thursday.

While ASML’s management affirmed long-term optimism, “the bookings miss sparked fresh debates about whether the AI-driven spending spree from giants like Google, Nvidia, and Microsoft might be cooling down,” said Jacob Falkencrone, global head of investment strategy at Saxo.

Semiconductor firms, which are highly sensitive to economic cycles, have borne the brunt of the market selloff this year. The chipmaker index has declined more than 22% in 2025, compared with a drop of 10% in the S&P 500.

The latest curbs on Nvidia’s exports to China marked another setback to an industry already battered by geopolitical tensions and a disrupted supply chain.

The H20 restrictions are “driven by concerns over China’s rise in the electronics sector, and in that sense, it is likely to become a permanent policy,” said Tomo Kinoshita, global market strategist at Invesco Asset Management. “It is expected to have a significant negative impact on the semiconductor supply chain.”

For China, the broader restrictions raise concerns that access to global tech hardware will be further choked off. Exports of the most-advanced chips and equipment to the Asian nation are already banned, and the H20 is a scaled-down product specifically designed not to be too powerful.

“It is a reminder of the potential susceptibility of tech stocks to the ongoing prickly relationship between the US and China regarding semiconductors,” said Tim Waterer, chief market analyst at KCM Trade in Sydney. “There is a reliance on the H20 chip from big name players in the Asian tech space, so any moves which could impact supply will be a drag on the broader sector.”

US-listed Chinese tech stocks were broadly lower, with Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. down 4.9% and Baidu Inc falling 2.4%.

On the other side of the equation, China continues to work toward self-reliance in technology, though its companies are still seen lagging far behind advanced global leaders like Nvidia.

Among Chinese hardware stocks bucking the regional sector declines Wednesday, Hua Hong Semiconductor Ltd closed up 0.6% in Hong Kong while Advanced Micro-Fabrication Equipment Inc rose 2.5% in Shanghai.

“AI innovation in China is booming and the H20 ban would not dampen it — it may accelerate the use of China domestic chips,” said Vey-Sern Ling, a managing director at Union Bancaire Privee. “Domestic AI chips may not perform as well as Nvidia’s H20, but that is missing the point. China has been able to develop innovative AI models despite US restrictions.”
